question: Using Table 5.2-5 and Table 5.2-4, what water chemistry limits apply to the primary coolant, and what materials must they protect?

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) Code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and associated Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC requirements, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0008.title: Compliance with Codes and Code Cases
nuscale_ch05_0008.summary: This section describes how the NuScale Power Module (NPM) and its reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) components comply with relevant U.S. regulations, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and associated General Design Criteria. It details that these components are designed, fabricated, and tested to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) Section III, 2013 Edition standards, and clarifies the classification of various piping and components according to their safety significance.

nuscale_ch05_0013.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0013.summary: This section lists references and standards used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report regarding the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary. It cites guidelines and reports from the Electric Power Research Institute and the American Society of Mechanical Engineers, as well as specific ASME code cases relevant to reactor component materials, inspection, and quality assurance.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component's functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to more detailed sections.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and is engineered to meet safety and performance requirements during normal and off-normal conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including low alloy steel for the baffle plate with stainless steel and nickel-based cladding for corrosion protection, and specifies the use of corrosion-resistant alloys for heater bundles, cover plates, and bolting. All materials are selected for compatibility with operating environments and comply with relevant BPVC Section II specifications.

nuscale_ch05_0010.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials
nuscale_ch05_0010.summary: The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) materials, including welds, are selected and fabricated in accordance with ASME BPVC standards and meet all relevant regulatory requirements, such as 10 CFR 50 and its appendices. These materials are designed, constructed, and tested to ensure high quality, environmental compatibility, and fracture resistance, with detailed specifications provided in Table 5.2-4.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance by establishing steady-state parameters—such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures—across different power levels using thermal hydraulic modeling and verification. It details how uncertainties and design considerations are addressed to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, particularly emphasizing the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

edges.description: Section A provides details about overpressure protection and references the use of ASME BPVC code cases for design and construction, while Section B establishes compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a and the requirement to use ASME BPVC Section III for the reactor coolant pressure boundary, thus Section A specifies implementation details for the general compliance framework described in Section B.

Section A discusses compliance with ASME and NRC regulatory codes for the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B lists references, including EPRI water chemistry guidelines, that inform the technical basis for those regulatory requirements; thus, Section A references the standards and requirements that are supported by the documents cited in Section B.

Section A establishes compliance with regulatory codes for reactor coolant boundary integrity, while Section B lists reference documents (including water chemistry guidelines) that inform or support the implementation of those codes, specifically regarding material protection requirements.

Section A lists system components and operational steps that require protection by water chemistry limits, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that set requirements for the integrity of those components, thus connecting the materials to be protected with the regulatory framework for their protection.

Section A describes the system components and operational procedures that require protection, while Section B specifies that these components must comply with regulatory codes (such as ASME BPVC) which include water chemistry limits to protect the integrity of the reactor coolant boundary materials.

Section A identifies specific pressurizer and reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials that require protection, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that set requirements—including water chemistry limits—to ensure the integrity of these materials.

Section A identifies specific pressurizer and reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials that require protection, while Section B establishes that these materials must comply with regulatory codes (ASME BPVC) which include requirements for water chemistry limits to ensure material integrity.

Section A identifies the specific pressurizer and reactor pressure vessel materials that require protection, while Section B cites industry guidelines and standards that establish the water chemistry limits necessary to protect those materials from degradation.

Section A describes the materials used for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) and their compliance with ASME and regulatory requirements, while Section B discusses the use of ASME code cases and regulatory provisions for overpressure protection, both referencing the ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a as the regulatory framework connecting material selection and protection requirements.

Section A specifies the materials and standards used for the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), while Section B establishes that these materials and components comply with regulatory requirements, specifically 10 CFR 50.55a and ASME BPVC Section III, thus Section A provides details for the general compliance described in Section B.

Section A describes the materials used for the reactor coolant pressure boundary that must be protected, while Section B lists references (such as EPRI water chemistry guidelines) that specify the water chemistry limits designed to protect those materials.

Section A discusses the establishment of primary coolant parameters for optimal system performance, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC) that the reactor coolant boundary materials must comply with, connecting the chemistry limits in A to the material protection requirements in B.

Section A discusses the establishment of steady state parameters for optimal primary coolant performance, while Section B specifies that the reactor coolant pressure boundary materials must comply with ASME Code requirements, thus the water chemistry limits in A are set to protect the materials identified in B.

Section A discusses the establishment of steady state parameters for primary coolant performance, while Section B cites references (such as EPRI water chemistry guidelines) that provide the regulatory basis for the water chemistry limits that must be applied to protect materials in the reactor coolant boundary.

Section A lists system components such as the pressurizer and chemical volume control system, while Section B provides a broader summary of the reactor coolant system and its major components, thus Section A specifies details for the general system described in Section B.

Section B specifies the materials used in the pressurizer and related reactor coolant system components, which Section A identifies as being exposed to primary coolant and thus requiring protection by water chemistry limits.

Section A describes system components and operational procedures that involve the primary coolant, while Section B specifies the materials used in the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) that must be protected by the water chemistry limits applied to the primary coolant.

Section B discusses the establishment of steady state values for primary and secondary side parameters (including water chemistry limits) to optimize system performance, while Section A describes system components (such as the chemical volume control system and steam generators) that must be protected by these water chemistry limits.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system and its components, while Section B specifies the materials used in the pressurizer, which is one of the components identified in Section A.

Section A describes the components of the reactor coolant system that use primary coolant, while Section B specifies the materials used in the reactor coolant pressure boundary that must be protected by water chemistry limits.

Section A describes the components of the reactor coolant system that require protection, while Section B discusses the establishment of operational parameters (such as water chemistry limits) to optimize system performance and protect those materials.

Section A identifies specific pressurizer components and refers to Section B for the description and regulatory requirements of the materials used in those components, establishing that the water chemistry limits must protect the materials listed in Section B.

Section A identifies specific pressurizer and reactor pressure vessel materials that are part of the primary coolant boundary and references Section 5.2 for material details, while Section B discusses the establishment of steady-state parameters (including water chemistry limits) for optimal system performance, thus connecting the need to protect the materials listed in Section A with the chemistry limits established in Section B.

Section A identifies the materials used in the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) that must be protected, while Section B discusses the establishment of operational parameters (such as water chemistry limits) to optimize system performance and protect those materials.
